coredata.dc:title: Visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes using a TiO2 and waste-based carbon dots nanocomposite

coredata.dc:description: Herein we report a visible-light-active photocatalytic nanocomposite (NC50:50) prepared from carbon dots (CDs) and TiO2 nanoparticles, which was applied to the photodegradation of organic dyes in water. The CDs incorporated corn stover, a major agricultural waste, and were prepared via hydrothermal treatment. Using a visible-light irradiation source and the dye methylene blue as a representative of the organic dyes class, we observed that a 374 % enhancement of the catalytic performance was achieved by adding CDs relative to bare TiO2. This was possible due to increased visible-light absorption and better photonic efficiency. Tests using reactive species scavengers indicated that three active species (superoxide anion, hydroxyl radicals, and electrons) were responsible for the photodegradation process, differing from bare TiO2 in which only the hydroxyl radical has a relevant role. Photocatalytic degradation was also observed toward Rhodamine B, Orange II and Methyl Orange. Finally, we performed a life cycle assessment (LCA) study to assess and analyse the associated environmental impacts of NC50:50 compared with other alternatives, which revealed that NC50:50 is the alternative resulting in the least environmental impacts. In summary, NC50:50 could, under visible-light irradiation, efficiently remove different organic dyes while incorporating organic waste materials and reducing the impacts associated with their use. We expect that this study provides a base for a more environmentally sustainable design of visible-light-active photocatalysts via waste upcycling.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis and purification of the CDs employed in the photocatalytic nanocomposite.

a) representation of -ln (Ct/C0) versus time of visible-light irradiation for NC50:50 and commercial TiO2; b) Ct/C0values for the visible-light-driven photodegradation of MB; c) COD analysis for MB samples prior to and after irradiation (240 minutes, visible-light) in the presence of NC50:50.

a) plot of -ln(Ct/C0) and b) Ct/C0values versus irradiation time for the degradation of MB, RhB, MO and Orange II with NC50:50; c) plot of -ln(Ct/C0) and d) Ct/C0 values versus visible-light irradiation time for the treatment of solution containing MB and RhB (2.5 mg L−1 each) with NC50:50; e) full visible-range absorption spectra for NC50:50-mediated MB/RhB samples degradation with increasing irradiation times; f) COD analysis for MB/RhB samples (2.5 mg L−1 each) prior to and after 240 minutes of irradiation with NC50:50.

Visible-light-driven NC50:50-mediated p photodegradation of MB in the absence or presence of scavengers for reactive species. Results are presented in the form of %DRV. A negative %DRV value is an indication that the catalytic efficiency was hindered by the respective scavengers when compared to a control without scavengers.

a) UV-Vis DRS spectra of prepared bare TiO2 (with annealing temperatures of 300 and 500 ºC), commercial TiO2, NC100 and NC50:50; b) steady-state photoluminescence spectra of bare TiO2 (annealing at 300 ºC), commercial TiO2 and NC50.50 and c) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of commercial TiO2 and NC50.50.
SEM images of NC50:50 at a) 200x and b) 25 000x magnification.
a) XRD pattern; b) FT-IR spectra of NC50:50; XPS core level spectra c) C 1 s d) O 1 s, e) N 1 s and f) Ti 2p of NC50:50.
a) 1H MAS (MAS rate: 30 kHz), b) 13C CP-MAS (MAS rate: 15 kHz) and c) 2D 1H-1H SQ-DQ (MAS rate: 30 kHz) spectra for the NC50:50.
The relative contribution of TTIP, IPA, CDs, and water toward different environmental impact categories of NC50:50 calculated using the ReCiPe2016 LCIA and a weight-based FU of 1 kg of catalyst.

Relative environmental profiles for TiO2, NC50:50 and NC100 obtained using ReCiPe 2016 v1.1 as LCIA. The comparison between the catalysts was made using a) a weight-based FU and b) a reaction rate constant-based FU.
Literature reports regarding the photodegradation of organic dyes by different TiO2-based nanocomposites under visible-light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes using a TiO2 and waste-based carbon dots nanocomposite

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein we report a visible-light-active photocatalytic nanocomposite (NC50:50) prepared from carbon dots (CDs) and TiO2 nanoparticles, which was applied to the photodegradation of organic dyes in water. The CDs incorporated corn stover, a major agricultural waste, and were prepared via hydrothermal treatment. Using a visible-light irradiation source and the dye methylene blue as a representative of the organic dyes class, we observed that a 374 % enhancement of the catalytic performance was achieved by adding CDs relative to bare TiO2. This was possible due to increased visible-light absorption and better photonic efficiency. Tests using reactive species scavengers indicated that three active species (superoxide anion, hydroxyl radicals, and electrons) were responsible for the photodegradation process, differing from bare TiO2 in which only the hydroxyl radical has a relevant role. Photocatalytic degradation was also observed toward Rhodamine B, Orange II and Methyl Orange. Finally, we performed a life cycle assessment (LCA) study to assess and analyse the associated environmental impacts of NC50:50 compared with other alternatives, which revealed that NC50:50 is the alternative resulting in the least environmental impacts. In summary, NC50:50 could, under visible-light irradiation, efficiently remove different organic dyes while incorporating organic waste materials and reducing the impacts associated with their use. We expect that this study provides a base for a more environmentally sustainable design of visible-light-active photocatalysts via waste upcycling.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The preservation of the drinkable water supply is of paramount importance for mankind. Thus, the contamination of Earth’s drinkable water supply could lead to severe water scarcity, a catastrophic outcome that could negatively impact global health, environmental, and economic standards [1,2]. Persistent organic pollutants are particularly important across the several classes of pollutants found in aquatic environments due to their long-lasting nature and possible damaging by-products [3]. Currently, conventional wastewater treatment methods frequently include physical adsorption, coagulation, and biodegradation, all of which can be ineffective when it comes to removing these persistent pollutants that include classes of commonly used compounds such as organic dyes and antibiotics [4,5].

More recently, methods based on advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), like the use of photocatalytic technologies, have been extensively studied as potential alternative treatment methodologies. Photocatalysis can be described as the promotion of a chemical reaction due to the action of a photocatalyst activated upon the absorption of suitable irradiation and can be achieved with different types of photocatalysts, such as semiconductors [6–10], carbon-based nanomaterials [11,12] or organophotocatalysts [13,14]. Compared to traditional water treatment methods, using photocatalytic technologies presents several advantages. Namely, it can be less environmentally impactful than traditional chemical oxidation due to the possibility of using an inexhaustible energy source (solar irradiation), may achieve a virtually complete removal of the target pollutants, and there is no generation of catalyst by-products during the photocatalytic process [15,16].

Amongst the photocatalysts currently known to the scientific community, titanium dioxide (TiO2) is particularly important. Not only is it the most widely used photocatalyst in today’s world, but it is also inexpensive, stable, and used for an array of purposes, including the photocatalytic removal of persistent pollutants such as textile dyes and pharmaceuticals (e.g. methylene blue (MB) and ibuprofen, respectively), heavy toxic metals photoreduction and removal, and hydrogen generation via water splitting [17–22]. However, TiO2 lacks in terms of efficient utilization of solar irradiation. This is due to its high energy bandgap (2.8–3.2 eV), limiting the photocatalytic region of the semiconductor to high-energy lower-wavelength radiation (λ < 400 nm) [23], which is around 6 % of the whole solar spectrum [24]. Furthermore, after photoexcitation, the semiconductor suffers from rapid charge carrier recombination, which reduces its photonic efficiency [25]. Finally, due to the composition of its surface, TiO2 presents a poor affinity towards organic pollutants [26].

Active research has been conducted to improve the absorption of radiation in the visible range and the efficiency of TiO2 under solar irradiation. Several methods have been tested and reported thus far, such as doping and coupling with metals/non-metals (e.g. platinum or nitrogen, respectively) [27,28], and conjugating the semiconductor with alternative materials, such as graphene [29,30]. Employing these methods led to a higher affinity towards organic pollutants, increasing the quantity of those reaching the active site and improving the activity of the catalysts under visible radiation due to higher stability of the nanocrystalline structure and enabling new energy levels [30–32]. However, in most cases, these techniques require expensive and highly toxic metal elements, resulting in a lower photonic efficiency, thus decreasing the catalytic potential [32].

Another possible alternative to address these limitations is conjugating the semiconductor with carbon dots (CDs). These nanometric carbon-based particles appear as spherical or quasi-spherical structures, including a sp2-conjugated core (either amorphous or crystalline) surrounded by a functionalized surface [33]. CDs display a variety of properties worth noting. Chief among these is, in terms of photocatalytic applications, high photoluminescence, a broad absorption extending into the visible range, good solubility in aqueous media, and low toxicity [34–36]. Due to being relatively inexpensive, easy to prepare, and presenting interesting properties, CDs are used for applications such as sensing [37], light-emitting devices [38], photodynamic therapy [39] and photocatalysis (either by themselves or in conjugation with semiconductors such as TiO2) [40–42].

The rationale behind the combination of TiO2 with CDs is that, unlike strategies such as doping or coupling, it overcomes the difficulties posed by using the semiconductor for photocatalysis without compromising in terms of additional limitations or toxicity [43]. CDs, having a lower cost and toxicity than the alternatives, do not hinder TiO2’s photonic efficiency nor induce harmful effects on human health or the environment [27,28,32]. In fact, due to their electron transfer properties and high electron storage capacity, they can accept the photoexcited e- from TiO2’s conduction band, preventing their recombination with h+ and promoting a better separation of the photoinduced charge carriers [44]. Furthermore, because of their enhanced absorption, they can expand the nanocomposite’s absorption toward the visible region, permitting a more efficient application when using solar irradiation [41,42]. Finally, the functionalized surface brings a higher affinity towards organic compounds, increasing pollutant adsorption and promoting access to the active sites [42].

Another advantage is that CDs can be prepared from a wide array of compounds, including using organic waste as carbon precursors. Thus, this type of material has the potential to address the problems associated with using TiO2 in real-world photocatalytic applications while upcycling an organic waste that would otherwise remain unutilized. The preparation of nano-catalysts from waste could also yield additional benefits in terms of reducing the environmental impacts of their preparation. This is particularly important given that, regarding CDs, Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) studies have demonstrated that the carbon precursor used during the synthesis may be a major factor impacting the resulting environmental impacts [45–47]. Thus, to minimize the impacts associated with the compound used as the carbon precursor, one could either reduce the amount or replace the commercial chemicals with waste materials as alternative precursors. One example of this would be to use corn stover as part of the carbon precursor mixture for the CDs. Corn stover, a major by-product of the corn industry, consists of the leaves, stalk and cobs of the corn plant left after harvest. It is a major agricultural waste that makes up about half of the corn crop’s yield, and of which only around 10 % is used in other applications [48]. Given its high volume, the upcycling of corn stover into a nanotechnology solution for wastewater treatment should be greatly appreciated.

Herein, we describe the making of visible-light-active photocatalytic nanocomposites prepared from TiO2 and corn stover-based CDs. This nanocomposite was tested for the photodegradation of several textile dyes, including MB. Tests employing reactive species scavengers were performed to assess the catalytic mechanism and identify the species involved in the process. Additionally, the nanocomposites were characterized using several techniques, including SEM, FT-IR, XPS, XRD, ss-NMR, EIS, UV-Vis, and photoluminescence spectroscopy. Finally, LCA studies were performed to investigate the environmental impacts of its preparation and use.

Titanium (IV) isopropoxide (TTIP, 99.99 %), citric acid (CA, 99.5 %), ethylenediamine (EDA, 99 %), sodium azide (99.5 %), Rhodamine B (95 %), p-benzoquinone, Orange II (85 %), and Titanium dioxide nanoparticles (99.7 %, <25 nm particle size, anatase phase) were acquired from Sigma Aldrich. Isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 99.9 %) was bought from Fisher Chemical. Potassium nitrate (99 %) and potassium iodide (99 %) were purchased from Merck. Methylene blue (MB) was obtained from Carlo Erba. Methyl orange was obtained from Honeywell Fluka. All commercially acquired reagents were utilized as they were received.

Corn stover was collected in an agricultural field in Mindelo, Portugal. Before using the material, corn stover was first washed with deionized water before drying at 50 ºC in a VWR DL 112 Prime oven (2500 W). Afterward, it was milled using a Laarmann CM50 mill and a sieve size of 0.5 mm. The resulting crushed corn stover was stored in a freezer (-80 ºC) to prevent the growth of microorganisms.

The CDs were prepared via a hydrothermal process (represented in Fig. 1) based on previous work by our team. Briefly, CA (0.075 g), corn stover (0.075 g), and EDA (166.9 µL) were mixed in a NaOH 0.01 M (10 mL). The resulting solution was inserted into a Teflon-lined vessel surrounded by a steel outer shell. Hydrothermal treatment ensued at 200 ºC during 8 h. After allowing the reaction product to cool down to room temperature, they were centrifuged (6000 rpm, 30 min) and dialyzed (3.5 kDa MWCO for 24 h) to remove both insoluble and soluble impurities, respectively, which could alter the CDs properties and reactivity [49]. Finally, the CDs solution was lyophilized, weighted and then dissolved in deionized water, resulting in a CDs solution of known concentration that was kept in the cold (4 ºC) until further use.

For the preparation of 0.5 g of bare TiO2, we followed a method previously used by our team [50,51]. We start by adding TTIP (1.855 mL) dropwise to IPA (11.975 mL) under stirring. After 30 min, deionized water was added (11.25 mL), precipitating TiO2. The mixture was then stirred for two more hours before being vacuum-filtered and dried overnight in an oven (VWR DL 112 Prime, 2500 W). Finally, the catalyst was powdered in a mortar and annealed at 300 ºC for 3 h (Thermolyne 47900 muffle, 1000 W). It was then stored in the dark until use.

For the preparation of the nanocomposites [52], the process was similar to that of TiO2, but with the addition of the necessary quantity of CDs amounting to 2–4 % of the total expected mass being added dropwise to the suspension prior to the vacuum-filtering stage and the 2 h of stirring before [52]. The CDs solution was kept under stirring at 70 ºC for 90 minutes before adding it to the TiO2 suspension. Two different nanocomposites were prepared over the course of this work. The first one, termed NC50:50, was prepared using the CDs described in the “2.2. CDs synthesis and purification” section, employing a CA – corn stover ratio of 50:50 as co-carbon precursors. The second composite, termed NC100, was prepared using CDs employing only a commercially available reagent, namely CA, as a carbon precursor, as reported in [52].

The visible-light-driven catalytic potential of TiO2, NC50:50 and NC100 was assessed using a MainHouse energy saving lamp (23 W, 6400 K, 1311 Lm) as the irradiation source, having this lamp already been employed in similar tests [51,53]. For the test, 2.5 mg L−1 of MB was photodegraded, with the catalytic efficiency being monitored via analysis of the absorbance at 665 nm (MB’s absorption peak, εm = 73460 M−1 cm−1) solution [54,55], Briefly, in a normal experimental setting, 20 mg of the photocatalyst was mixed with 100 mL of MB solution (2.5 mg L−1 in H2O). Afterward, the solution was put in an ultrasonic bath for 1 min to disperse the catalyst and, before switching the irradiation source on, was kept under magnetic stirring in the dark for 30 min to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium [56]. Then, the lamp was switched on, and aliquots were collected 30 min before irradiation (t = -30, used to measure the adsorption of the pollutant by the catalysts), at the initial time (t = 0) and every additional 30 min of irradiation time. Before analysis via UV-Vis spectrophotometry, the aliquots were centrifuged (10,000 rpm for 10 min) to separate the heterogeneous catalyst from the treated sample. Tests with dyes other than MB were also made, with their respective concentrations being adjusted so that A = 0.5 at their absorption peak.

To evaluate the role of each reactive species during the photodegradation of MB, tests were made employing scavengers for different free radicals’ reactive species, with the effects of the presence of these scavengers in the catalytic mixture being monitored. Namely, hydroxyl radicals (·OH), superoxide radicals (O2 ·-), electrons (e-), holes (h+) and singlet oxygen (1O2) were scavenged using isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 10 mM) [53], p-Benzoquinone (1 mM) [57], potassium nitrate (KNO3, 10 mM) [58], potassium iodide (KI, 10 mM) [53], and sodium azide (1 mM) [59], respectively. For these tests, all reactional conditions were as described in the “Photocatalytic activity assays” (240 min of irradiation). Finally, the pollutant removal rate was compared to that of control tests performed without any scavenger.

The morphology of bare TiO2 and NC50:50 was obtained via high-resolution scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Namely, a FEI Quanta 400 ESEM FEG system was used to analyze powder samples scattered in a carbon tape. Average sizes for the catalysts were obtained by calculating the average size of 100 individually measured particles.

The photocatalysts’ absorption profiles were recorded using UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS). The equipment used for this was a Jasco V-760 spectrophotometer with an integration sphere (Jasco ILV-924) addon. Barium sulfate was used as the reference.

Photoluminescence spectra were collected via a Horiba Jovin Yvon Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer in standard 10 nm fluorescence quartz cells. The data was processed and analyzed via the FluorEssence software provided with the equipment.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out using a computer-controlled VSP-300 multichannel potentiostat from BioLogic, controlled with EC-Lab V11.26 software. A screen-printed carbon electrode (C-SPE DropSens, reference DRP-110) was obtained from Metrohm Autolab (Utrecht, Netherlands). Electrochemical cleaning of the electrode was carried out using a 0.5 M H2SO4 aqueous solution and using the cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique (until it reached a profile i = f(t) stable over time). An average of 15 cycles was considered at a speed rate of 0.1 V s−1, using an interval of potential of −0.45 to + 1.05 V vs. Ag. Once this procedure was completed, the electrodes were thoroughly washed with ultrapure water. To obtain the working electrode, 5 mg of samples were suspended in 0.5 mL deionized water with 0.05 mL of 5 wt% Nafion 117 dispersion, and then the mixture was sonicated for 30 min and spread onto the WE of the SPE. 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte. A MainHouse energy saving lamp (23 W, 6400 K, 1311 Lm) was used as the light source. The EIS measurements were performed applying a sinusoidal signal of 10 mV (rms) and with frequencies logarithmically distributed from 20 kHz to 1 Hz. The measurements were taken at 0.50 V.

Infrared spectra were collected using a Vertex70 (Bruker) FT-IT spectrophotometer coupled to a Specac Golden Gate Single Reflection Diamond Attenuated Total Reflectance addon. The spectra were analyzed in the range of 4000–500 cm−1 with a spectral resolution of 4 cm−1.

X-Ray powder diffraction (XRPD) patterns were obtained using an automated diffractometer (PANanalatytical EMPYREAN) and recorded between 5 and 70 in 2θ (1 h of measuring time) in Bragg-Brentano reflection configuration with a PIXcel 3D detector with a step size of 0.017º (2θ).

XPS analysis was made using a Physical Electronics PHI VersaProbe II spectrometer employing monochromatic Al-Ka radiation (49.1 W, 15 kV and 1486.6 eV) with a hemispherical multichannel detector. A constant pass energy value of 29.35 eV and a 200 μm circular analysis area were used to record the spectra, which were then analyzed via the PHI SmartSoft software and processed using MultiPak 9.3 package. The adventitious carbon C 1 s signal (284.8 eV) was used as a reference for the binding energy values. Shirley-type background and Gauss-Lorentz curves were used to determine the binding energies.

Solid-state Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (ss-NMR) experiments were performed using a spectrometer equipped with a 14.1 T narrow bore magnet operating at Larmor frequencies of 600.09 MHz and 150.91 MHz for 1H and 13C, respectively. A powdered sample was packed in a 2.5 mm ZrO2 rotor and rotated at room temperature at magic angle spinning (MAS) rates of 15 or 30 kHz. The recycling time was set at 5 s with a contact time during the cross-polarization of 2 ms for 13C spectrum. 1H-MAS and 2D homonuclear correlation experiments were recorded in a 2.5 mm MAS probe at a MAS rate of 30 kHz. The 2D 1H-1H SQ-DQ homonuclear correlation experiment was recorded using the back-to-back (BaBa) pulse sequence with excitation and reconversion times of two rotor periods. Chemical shifts (δ) for 1H and 13C (expressed in ppm) are relative to TMS (δ1H = 0 ppm) and adamantane (δ13C = 38.5 ppm), respectively.

This cradle-to-gate LCA study aimed to compare the relative environmental results from the production of TiO2, NC100, and NC50:50. A laboratory-scale manufacturing of the photocatalysts was considered for this study, including the direct emissions and upstream impacts associated with resource extraction. Regarding the electricity used in the process, its impacts were not considered, as they are similar for the three processes. The production processes considered for TiO2, NC100, and NC50:50 were as described in the “2.3. TiO2/CDs nanocomposites preparation” section, building on a previous work by our team [52]. We started by analyzing and comparing the environmental impacts based on a functional unit (FU) of 1 kg of produced material. Selecting a weight -based FU is useful to compare the impacts associated with producing an equivalent quantity of similar nanomaterials [60,61]. Afterwards, the impacts were analyzed by considering a function-based FU depending on the reaction rate constants achieved by each catalyst. This is necessary given that the 1 kg FU cannot take into account that sometimes a more impactful process could be more beneficial due to higher applicability and functionality of the resulting material. In our case, given the intended purpose for which our nanocomposites were prepared, we chose the reaction rate constant as a measure of the functionality of our catalysts.

The LCA study was made using inventory (foreground) data inputs based on the process described in the “Materials and methods” section, with the foreground system using the database information from Ecoinvent® 3.9.1 for IPA (Isopropanol {RER}| market for isopropanol | Cut-off, S) and deionized water (Water, deionized, from tap water, at user {Europe without Switzerland} | market for). Additionally, some processes were not available in the database and were created by us based on information provided by us or found in the literature. Namely, for the CDs, the process was created based on a methodology we developed and used, whereas for TTIP we obtained the information required from ref [62]. A summary of the background data used in each case can be found in Table S1.

For this study, all three catalysts (TiO2, NC50:50 and NC100) required the same electricity input during their synthesis, and so, the comparison between the relative environmental impacts of each catalyst does not depend on this parameter. Furthermore, a laboratory scale was considered for the purpose of our study, meaning that none of the equipment (such as the oven or furnace) was used to its full potential. Given this, providing an accurate calculation of the energy required by the processes is difficult, as the equipment used is inefficient when processing small quantities. As our objective with this study is to make a comparison between the relative impacts of the three different processes, and the fact that the processes have similar energy requirements, the contribution of the impacts associated to this factor was not included in the present LCA study [52].

The environmental impacts evaluated in this LCA study were modeled using the ReCiPe 2016 v1.1 LCIA, Hierarchist Version. The midpoint indicators taken into consideration in the ReCiPe method were: global warming – human health (GW - HH), global warming – terrestrial ecosystems (GW – TE), global warming – freshwater ecosystems (GW – FE), stratospheric ozone depletion (SO), ionization radiation (IR), ozone formation - human health (OF - HH), fine particulate matter formation (FPM), ozone formation - terrestrial ecosystems (OF-TE), terrestrial acidification (TA), freshwater eutrophication (FE), marine eutrophication (ME), terrestrial ecotoxicity (TET), freshwater ecotoxicity (FET), marine ecotoxicity (MET), human carcinogenic toxicity (HC), human non-carcinogenic toxicity (HNC), land use (LU), mineral resource scarcity (MR), fossil resource scarcity (FR), water consumption – human health (WC – HH), water consumption – terrestrial ecosystems (WC – TE) and water consumption – aquatic ecosystems (WC – AE). The LCA study was performed by using the SimaPro 9.5.0.0 software.

Detailed information about the nanocomposite preparation may be found on the Supporting Information (SI). Bare TiO2 nanoparticles were obtained from titanium (IV) isopropoxide (TTIP) in a sol-gel process and were analyzed previously by us in terms of photodegradation efficiency and characterization [52]. Hydrothermally-prepared CDs were made from a mixture of corn stover, citric acid (both as co-carbon precursors), and ethylenediamine (as N-dopant), with a mass ratio of 0.5:0.5:1, respectively. The TiO2-CDs nanocomposite (NC50:50) was prepared similarly to bare TiO2, but with addition of the CDs‘ solution during the last stage of synthesis process, prior to annealing. In identifying the NC, 50:50 refers to the mass ratio of corn stover to citric acid used as co-carbon precursors for the waste-based CDs.

The visible-light-driven photodegradation potential was first analysed by measuring the photocatalytic degradation of MB via UV-Vis spectrophotometry. In an early stage, to optimize the system, preliminary tests (20 mg of photocatalyst, 2.5 mg L−1 MB, 60 minutes of irradiation) were made to decide the ratio of corn stover - citric acid to be incorporated and the hydrothermal reaction time to be used in the making of the CDs for inclusion in photocatalytic nanocomposites. The effect of varying the wt% of CDs including in the nanocomposite and the annealing stage temperature were also tested this way. To compare these results to those of bare TiO2, we quantified the amount of pollutant removed over a set irradiation time (%DR, Eq. 1)[12] and calculated the associated rate constant (Eq. 2). To calculate the %DR, we employed the following equation:

Here, %DR corresponds to the % of textile dye removed over a set time of irradiation, C0 represents the initial concentration of MB in the mixture, and Ct is the concentration of MB after an irradiation period of t minutes. The dye concentration was obtained via analysis of the absorbance at 665 nm (εm = 73460 M−1 cm−1)[54,55] via UV-Vis spectrophotometry.

Furthermore, the kinetic equation of the degradation follows a pseudo-first-order reaction that can be written as follows [63]:

For the first set of preliminary assays, CDs were prepared with both varying corn stover-citric acid ratios and different reaction times. After synthesis and purification, the CDs were conjugated with TiO2, originating the catalytic nanocomposites. In terms of corn stover - citric acid ratio, we observed that, when using the same synthesis conditions (200 ºC during 24 h), a 50:50 ratio yielded the best results (Table S2), both in terms of rate constant (0.0051 versus 0.0025 and 0.0036 min−1) and %DR (26.4 versus 12.6 and 19.4 %). Considering this, we chose the 50:50 ratio. We tested the effects of different CDs’ hydrothermal reaction times on the resulting nanocomposites, with the respective results indicating that an 8 h reaction time originates the CDs with the highest potential to be incorporated in a catalytic nanocomposite (Table S3). The respective nanocomposites, incorporating CDs made using a 50:50 ratio and 8 h of reaction time, presented a reaction rate constant of 0.0111 ± 0.0012 min−1 coupled to a %DR of 48.4 ± 3.7 %.

Afterward, we also evaluated the wt% of CDs to be integrated into the nanocomposites. We have opted for a 4 wt% CDs inclusion, given that it yielded a slightly higher rate constant and %DR (0.121 ± 0.0006 min−1 and 51.5 ± 1.7 %, respectively) when compared to a 2 wt% concentration (0.0111 ± 0.0012 min−1 and 48.4 ± 3.7 %). Finally, the effect of the nanocomposite’s annealing temperature was also studied (Table S4). For temperatures between 300 and 500 ºC, we observed that the catalytic performance of the nanocomposite worsens as the temperature is gradually increased. As an example, a 300 ºC annealing temperature results in a rate constant and %DR (0.121 ± 0.0006 min−1 and 51.5 ± 1.7 %, respectively) more than 3 times higher than those resulting from a 350 ºC annealing temperature (0.0029 ± 0.0002 min−1 and 16.0 ± 1.1 %). This could be due to the gradual degradation of the CDs in the nanocomposite at higher annealing temperatures, which removed the effect provided by the presence of the CDs.

Based on the preliminary studies, the following conditions were chosen for the nanocomposite preparation: CDs produced via hydrothermal treatment (200 ºC for 8 h) using a 50:50 corn stover - citric acid ratio as carbon precursors mixed into TiO2 prepared from TTIP, 4 wt% of CDs incorporated into TiO2, annealing temperature of 300 ºC, hereon described as NC50:50. The corresponding nanocomposite resulted in a rate constant of 0.121 ± 0.0006 min−1 and %DR of 51.5 ± 1.7 % for an irradiation period of 60 minutes, which are significantly superior to those observed for similarly prepared bare TiO2 previously analysed by our team (from TTIP, annealing at 300 ºC, without the addition of CDs), 0.0023 ± 0.0003 min−1 and 16.6 ± 1.6 %, respectively [52]. Photocatalytic tests concerning TiO2 annealed at 500 ºC (0.0025 ± 0.0003 min−1 and 14.7 ± 1.4 %) and commercially available TiO2 nanoparticles (0.0024 ± 0.0004 min−1 and 15.4 ± 1.2 %) further confirmed the increments obtained upon adding CDs. In summary, these results suggest that adding CDs to TiO2 significantly increased the semiconductor’s catalytic activity under visible-light irradiation and that additional tests are warranted.

Following that, the efficiency of NC50:50 was compared to that of a benchmark consisting of commercially available TiO2 nanoparticles over 5 h of visible-light irradiation ( Fig. 2 a and b ). First and foremost, NC50:50 is superior to commercial TiO2 regarding the reaction rate constant and %DR. Namely, NC50:50 achieved a rate constant of 0.00949 min−1 versus 0.00192 min−1 for TiO2. Regarding %DR, NC50:50 managed to degrade 90.8 % of MB (240 min of irradiation), whereas TiO2 had only removed 44.4 % after 300 min. Not only that, but NC50:50 also presents higher rate constants than bare TiO2 prepared in the same conditions as the nanocomposite and NC100 (containing CDs made solely of citric acid and ethylenediamine), which were 0.00200 and 0.00934 min−1, as observed in a previous work [52]. The effect of photolysis is quite limited in these catalytic conditions, with a %DR of 35.5 % (360 min). Photodegradation in the presence of the CDs yielded similar results, suggesting that the CDs alone are insufficient to degrade MB in the same conditions.

To better evaluate the effect that adding CDs to TiO2 has on the photocatalyst’s %DR and reaction rate constant, we have calculated the extension of the enhancement of the catalytic efficiency induced by the addition of CDs via Eq. 3:

In this case, % ln(C) is a comparative measure of the enhancement of TiO2’s catalytic efficiency upon adding CDs, k
NC is the reaction rate constant obtained with the catalytic nanocomposite, and k
TiO2 is the reaction rate constant of TiO2 under similar conditions (annealing at 300 ºC). Regarding NC50:50, its calculated % ln(C) was 374 %, indicating that not only was there a significant performance enhancement upon adding the corn stover-containing CDs, but also that the catalytic performance was maintained even with the inclusion of an organic waste source as a co-carbon precursor for the CDs (using commercial reagents for CDs resulted in % ln(C) of 367 %) [52]. The pollutant removal was also evaluated via chemical oxygen demand (COD) analysis, which measures the oxygen necessary to fully oxidize the organic component found in contaminated aqueous samples and was already employed to quantify the degradation of MB from water-based samples [64,65]. COD analysis revealed a significant reduction in the oxidizable content after 240 minutes of irradiation (Fig. 2 c), with the results demonstrating the removal of 82 % of the initial MB concentration, which is similar to the 90.8 % observed via spectrophotometry. Furthermore, a comparison between the absorption profiles of the samples before and after 240 minutes of visible-light irradiation (Figure S1) shows a clear decrease in MB’s absorption peak. The absence of new peaks forming during the photodegradation suggests that no UV-Vis-absorbing by-products resulting are formed during MB’s photodegradation.

NC50:50 was also tested for the photodegradation of RhB, MO, and Orange II (same catalytic conditions but adjusting the concentration of each dye so that its absorbance was 0.5 at the maximum absorption wavelength) ( Fig. 3 a and b). First, it is worth noting that NC50:50 could degrade all of the tested organic dyes under irradiation with visible light, albeit with varying catalytic potentials. In terms of rate constant (Fig. 3 a), NC50:50 was most effective for the degradation of MB (0.00949 min−1), followed by RhB (0.00627 min−1), Orange II (0.00328 min−1), and finally, MO (0.00168 min−1). The corresponding %DRs (Fig. 3 b), 90.8, 81.7, 67.9, and 37.0, respectively, corroborated this.

Finally, to evaluate how NC50:50 would perform in a more complex sample, we analysed its potential to photodegrade an aqueous mixture of MB and RhB (both at a 2.5 mg L−1 concentration). We observed that, under the same conditions, NC50:50 maintained a good catalytic potential, achieving rate constants of 0.00810 and 0.00188 min−1 (Fig. 3 c), respectively, with corresponding %DRs of 89.1 and 43.7 % (Fig. 3 d). These results are quite interesting for two different reasons. First, even though we are now degrading a solution containing double the concentration of dyes (5 mg L−1 for MB and RhB together instead of 2.5 mg L−1 for tests with a single dye), NC50:50 almost matched the rate constant and %DR obtained for MB when the dye was alone (0.00949 vs 0.00810 min−1 and 90.8 vs 89.1 %, respectively), suggesting that NC50:50 retains its catalytic power even in more complex samples. Secondly, while the degradation parameters for MB remained nearly unchanged, this was not the case for RhB, whose %DR dropped from 81.7 % to 43.7 %. The degradation was considerably inhibited for this dye, as observed clearly by the different decrease of the absorption peaks corresponding to RhB and MB (Fig. 3 e). This suggests a preference by NC50:50 regarding the degradation targets, indicating that despite degrading both dyes, there is preference and selectivity toward MB when both dyes are together. Finally, the degradation was also assessed via COD (Fig. 3 f), which indicated a 60 % decrease of the pollutants in the sample after irradiation.

To gain insight into the reactive species behind the improved catalytic activity of NC50:50, MB photodegradation experiments were made in the presence of free radical species. Namely, hydroxyl radicals (·OH), superoxide radicals (O2 ·-), electrons (e-), holes (h+) and singlet oxygen (1O2) were scavenged using isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 10 mM), p-Benzoquinone (1 mM), potassium nitrate (KNO3, 10 mM), potassium iodide (KI, 10 mM), and sodium azide (1 mM), respectively. The scavengers employed were chosen because the reactive species they target commonly participate in the catalytic mechanism of TiO2 and its respective composites [43]. IPA and p-Benzoquinone are worthy of particular attention given that their respective target reactive species, hydroxyl and superoxide radicals, respectively, are the two most relevant species involved in bare TiO2’s mechanism [53,57].

The impact of each reactive species was gauged by quantifying the change in the %DR in the presence of the tested scavengers and is calculated as follows (Eq. 4 ) [52] :
(4)
% DRV =
% DR
test
−
% DR
control
Here, %DRV corresponds to the %DR variation when a scavenger (%DRtest) is added compared to a control test (%DRcontrol, standard photodegradation with the catalyst).

The test regarding the NC50:50 composites ( Fig. 4) yielded different outcomes depending on the reactive species tested. Tests using KI and sodium azide did not display any difference when compared to the control test (absence of scavengers), suggesting that their respective reactive species, h+ and 1O2, are not a part of the catalytic mechanism of NC50:50. On the contrary, the %DR was considerably reduced in the presence of IPA (-14.5 ± 0.5 %), p-Benzoquinone (-17.6 ± 1.0 %), and KNO3 (-12.8 ± 0.6 %). This demonstrates that their respective reactive species (hydroxyl radicals, superoxide radicals, and e-, respectively) are part of the photocatalytic mechanism and that their absence due to the scavengers negatively impacted the photocatalytic performance of the nanocomposite. Finally, it is worth mentioning that photolysis alone can be responsive for up to 20 % of %DR, and so we must keep this in mind when analyzing the results of the scavengers’ test.

In summary, under visible-light irradiation, three reactive species (·OH, O2 ·- and e-) are responsible for the photocatalytic mechanism of the nanocomposite. When one or more of those species are scavenged, the photodegradation efficiency is negatively affected. Out of the three species, a slight prominence of the superoxide radical role is found compared to the other two species.

Interestingly, these results differ from those of another TiO2/CDs nanocomposite. Namely, we have previously prepared one such nanocomposite (NC100), and tested it toward MB degradation, in which the CDs had only citric acid (sole carbon precursor) and ethylenediamine as precursors [52]. In this case, the superoxide anion was the main contributor (%DRV of −29.3 ± 0.4 %) for the photodegradation, with only a small fraction of it being attributed to hydroxyl radical (%DRV of −5.6 ± 0.5 %) [52]. As for bare TiO2, hydroxyl radicals were the main species behind the photodegradation mechanism, as previously observed [52].

This dominance of superoxide-driven photodegradation aligns well with previous studies concerning TiO2/CDs nanocomposites using CDs made fully from commercially available reagents [66,67]. As for NC50:50, including organic waste as part of the CDs led to a decrease in the predominance of the superoxide radical and the rise of the influence of the hydroxyl radical and the e-.

This emphasizes the sustainable development photocatalytic TiO2/CDs nanocomposites with high visible-light efficiency. To evaluate the efficiency of our system, NC50:50’s performance was compared with comparable composites available in the literature for the same purpose (MB photodegradation). The comparison was made in terms of % ln(C) ( Table 1), as this value considers the benefits obtained with the addition of the CDs while also accounting for the variability of the conditions on which the different experiments may have been conducted (e.g. catalyst dosage, pollutant concentration, lamp intensity, among others). To make the results more comparable, for multi-component systems, the % ln(C) calculation was made considering only the presence and absence of CDs, ignoring the effect conferred by other components (e.g., TiO2–Fe2O3–CDs versus TiO2–Fe2O3, respectively). From the data available (Table 1), we observe that NC50:50 achieved a very significant enhancement of the catalytic efficiency despite including corn stover as a co-carbon precursor. When compared to other works in the literature, most studies presented % ln(C) between 64 % and 233 %, which was way below the 374 % enhancement obtained with NC50:50. Aside from our own study, only one other team reported a close % ln(C) of 366 %, but that was using a composite including a third component (Fe2O3) that required a more resource- and time-intensive synthesis [67].

After observing the enhancement of catalytic performance obtained with NC50:50 compared to bare TiO2, it is now important to characterize catalysts to gain better insight regarding the basis of the superior performance obtained upon adding CDs. The CDs used for the making of NC50 : 50 were prepared via a methodology previously developed by our laboratory, and their complete characterization may be found in [71].

We have started by comparing the photocatalysts in terms of optical properties. First, we investigated the optical absorption profile of bare TiO2 (annealing stages at 300 and 500 ºC), commercial TiO2, NC100, and NC50:50, which includes corn stover as a co-carbon precursor for the CDs. By analyzing Fig. 5 a, we can easily observe that the three types of TiO2 present roughly the same profile, with TiO2’s absorption being limited to the UV range and an absorption edge at roughly 510 nm. The introduction of CDs alters the absorption profile, with both NC100 and NC50:50, presenting an enhanced absorption between 400 and 600 nm. For NC50:50, this effect is even more pronounced, with the nanocomposite absorbing radiation up to 700 nm and presenting a clear advantage regarding visible-light absorption. As for the reason behind the enhanced absorption in the visible region, reports have pointed out that Ti3+ ions can appear due to electron interchange with CDs, originating an enhanced absorption at λ > 600 nm [19]. For NC50:50, there is a small, yet observable, absorption at wavelengths over 600 nm by NC50:50, suggesting the presence of residual Ti3+ ions increases absorption at longer wavelengths. The enhanced absorption between 400 and 600 nm may be ascribed to the presence of the CDs themselves. Thus, either directly or indirectly (by originating Ti3+ ions), the CDs can improve the utilization of visible light by the nanocomposites, positively affecting its catalytic efficiency. Furthermore, the DRS data was used to calculate the band gap energies via Tauc’s formula ((αhν)2 = hν), where α and hν correspond to the absorbance and the energy of the photon at the corresponding wavelength, respectively). The calculated band gaps for TiO2, 300 ºC, and commercial, and NC50:50 are 3.32, 3.30, and 3.26 eV, respectively. This indicates that despite an improved visible irradiation uptake, the nanocomposite’s bandgap remained unchanged.

The photoluminescence spectra of bare TiO2 (300 ºC), commercial TiO2 and NC50:50 was recorded and analysed to uncover the role of the CDs regarding the inhibition of the recombination of TiO2’s photoinduced charge carriers. As observable in Fig. 5 b, TiO2 and the nanocomposites present a similar wide emission profile (350–600 nm). However, the addition of the CDs led to a significantly lower emission intensity in the respective nanocomposites when compared to TiO2. This suggests that the interaction between TiO2 and CDs results in a rapid transference of e- via an electron transfer interface occurring between the CDs and TiO2 [52,72]. Given that after electron transfer, the CDs may act as a reservoir and retain the electron, the recombination of TiO2’s e- and h+ is prevented. As a result the catalytic process is favoured. The better catalytic efficiency and inhibition of the recombination means that there is less energy dissipated as radiation, resulting in a lower emission intensity, which is corroborated by previous reports[73,74]. Finally, the smaller impedance arc radius presented by NC50:50 compared to commercial TiO2 (Fig. 5 c) indicates that the nanocomposite has a lower electron transfer resistance under visible-light irradiation. This, alongside the suggested improved optical absorption and delayed recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, demonstrates that adding the CDs can significantly promote the charge transfer and separation process.

To evaluate the morphology of NC50:50, the catalyst was observed via SEM ( Fig. 6). In line with other TiO2-based compounds, NC50:50 presented a rounded shape and an average size of 92.9 ± 16.5 nm [6,75]. Compared to similarly prepared bare TiO2 (≈140 nm) [52], the smaller size of the nanocomposite may contribute towards a better catalytic efficiency upon the addition of CDs, as it was observed that for TiO2, smaller sizes result in better catalytic efficiency [76]. Furthermore, NC50:50 displays a certain tendency towards forming aggregates, which the CDs’ functionalized surface may cause. Carboxyl, carbonyl, and amine groups, among others, are frequently present on these nanoparticles and may lead to electrostatic interactions and form hydrogen bonds between themselves, thus causing an agglomeration of particles [77].

The XRD patterns of NC50:50 were acquired and examined (Fig. 6 a). The XRD pattern showed that NC50:50 has the anatase crystal planes at (101) 25.12º, (004) 38.02º, (200) 47.83º, (105) 54.05º and (211) 54.98º and (204) 62.74º diffraction planes respectively [52,78]. Because of the great dispersion and low CDs content in NC50:50, the usual diffraction peaks of CDs were not adequately seen [79]. FT-IR spectra was recorded for NC50:50 and presented the expected signals (Fig. 6 b). A small shoulder at 1629 cm−1 was attributed to carbonyl groups [78], while the wide band centered at 3311 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of -OH groups, confirming that carboxylic acid was the main group found on NC50:50’s surface [80]. Finally, ∼600 cm−1 we find a peak that can be attributed to the vibration of Ti-O-Ti bonds, which was not surprising considering the anatase structure described previously [80].

The surface of NC50:50 was studied via XPS spectroscopy. We found that 46.5 and 30.4 % of it was O 1 s and C 1 s, respectively, as the main components, followed by a moderate content of Ti 2p, 22.2 %, and traces of N 1 s (0.8 %). When deconvoluted, the C 1s core level ( Fig. 7 c) revealed a major contribution (284.8 eV) attributed to the C-C/C-H bonds (89.9 %). The two bands found at 286.5 eV and 288.4 eV (C-N/C-O (5.84 %) and OC-O (4.27 %) bonds, respectively), together with the high percentage of oxygen found (46.5 %) confirmed the existence of a surface covered with highly oxidized groups. Supporting this, the analysis and deconvolution of the O 1 s (Fig. 7 d) presented bands at 530.1 (86.23 %) and 531.6 eV (13.77 %) ascribed to C–O/C–OH and carboxyl groups (-CO), respectively (Fig. 7 b). A small contribution from the N 1 s was observed at 400.1 eV related to the amine groups (Fig. 7 e). Regarding the Ti 2p core level spectra, two bands were observed at 458.6 and 464.5 eV, being assigned to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 spin-orbital splitting photoelectrons, respectively (Fig. 7 f).

To obtain information regarding the organic composition present in the nanocomposite at the atomic level, ss- 1H- and 13C NMR studies were carried out. The 1H MAS ss-NMR spectrum ( Fig. 8 a) for the nanoparticles showed two main signals usually found in TiO2 materials at a proton chemical shift (δ1H) of ∼0.7 and 5.5 ppm assigned to titanol groups and adsorbed water molecules, respectively [81,82]. The 13C-CP-MAS spectrum shows the chemical surrounding of CD nanoparticles conjugated to TiO2 nanocomposites (Fig. 8 b). The organic content was mainly attributed to sp 3 hybridization carbon atoms, as determined by the NMR signal at a 13C chemical shift (δ13C) located between 0 and 80 ppm, being the signals at 80–60 ppm and 50–0 ppm assigned to oxygenated (>C(O)-, >CH-O- or -CH2-OH) and aliphatic hydrocarbons (CH3-, -CH2-, >CH- or >C<), respectively. Besides, two low-intensity 13C resonance signals were present at 185 and 182 ppm, which were ascribed to carboxylic acid carbons. The latter results agree with those obtained in XPS and FTIR experiments. Interestingly, important bulk chemical information was obtained even with a low signal-to-noise ratio in the 13 C CP-MAS spectrum. Thus, the highly oxygenated aliphatic structure can be proposed for covering the CDs conjugated to the novel TiO2 nanocomposite. Commonly, the sp 2-conjugated core of the CD structure is observed neither with 13C CP-MAS nor with direct polarization experiments.

2D 1H ss-NMR experiments were performed for NC50:50 (Fig. 8 c). Less intense 1H resonance signals from the hydrocarbon chain were observed at δ1H of 1.5 and 3.5 ppm, which are related to the 13C ss-NMR signals at δ13C of 50–10 and 80–60 ppm, respectively. Furthermore, 2D 1H-1H SQ-DQ experiments were done to increase the resolution of the proton dimension. In this experiment, additional 1H contributions were observed at high-frequency values. These contributions were associated with acidic bridging hydroxyl groups at 6–9 ppm of the TiO2 particles [81,82]. Interestingly, the presence of the A-B correlation between the titanol group at a δ1H of 0.9–0.6 ppm and the oxygenated aliphatic regions (δ1H of 3–4 ppm) from the covering of the CDs demonstrate the presence of the nanocomposite arising from the short-range interactions between TiO2 and CDs determined by the 2D 1H-1H SQ-DQ experiment. Besides, other cross-peaks (A-D and A-E correlations) were present between the titanol and other OH groups of the TiO2 particles.

Considering our intended target of developing a more environmentally sustainable photocatalytic nanocomposite, the evaluation of the environmental impacts is an important step that needs to be considered to assess if adding corn stover as a co-carbon precursor had an overall beneficial effect. An LCA study was performed to evaluate if enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2 by adding CDs is worth the possibility of potentially increasing the impacts associated with the inclusion of the carbon nanoparticles and if partially utilizing corn stover instead of citric acid decreases the overall environmental impacts. For this purpose, the impacts associated with producing NC50:50 were evaluated regarding midpoint impacts. Furthermore, the relative impacts of NC50:50 were compared to those of NC100 and bare TiO2. A weight-based functional unit (FU) was used in the first stage, followed by a function-based FU based on the rate constant of their respective photocatalyst, which was used in a later stage.

We began our study by evaluating the impacts associated with NC50:50 using a FU of 1 kg of catalyst produced ( Fig. 9). Herein, we also compared the relative contributions of individual inputs for the impact categories tested for TiO2, NC50:50, and NC100. Impacts associated with energy usage during the preparation of the photocatalyst were not considered, as they would not be accurate for laboratory-scale studies and would not impact a comparison between NC50:50, NC100, and TiO2, as the three processes require the same electricity-consuming steps. Regarding NC50:50, the environmental impacts result mostly from TTIP and IPA for nearly all impact categories, apart from the marine eutrophication category, for which the CDs are the largest contributor. The categories of stratospheric ozone depletion, freshwater eutrophication, freshwater ecotoxicity, marine ecotoxicity, human carcinogenic toxicity, land use, and mineral resource scarcity are especially affected by TTIP. On the other hand, IPA contributes significantly to global warming (human health, terrestrial ecosystems, and freshwater ecosystems), ionization radiation, ozone formation (human health and terrestrial ecosystems), fine particular matter formation, terrestrial acidification, terrestrial ecotoxicity, fossil resource scarcity and water consumption (human health, terrestrial ecosystems, and aquatic ecosystems). When compared to similarly prepared bare TiO2, published in ref [52], the impact distribution of NC50:50 shows a mostly small contribution of the CDs towards all impact categories (and a major contribution towards marine eutrophication) which is not present in TiO2 [52]. When compared to the results of NC100, published in ref [52], we can see that, by employing corn stover as a co-carbon precursor and by relying in a lower hydrothermal reaction time (8 h versus 24 h), despite using 4 wt% instead of 2 wt% of CDs, the impacts ascribed to the presence of CDs in NC50:50 are smaller than those found for the CDs in NC100.

To facilitate the comparison between NC50:50 and bare TiO2/NC100, we have compared regarding endpoint indicators, namely human health, ecosystems, and resources. First, when using a weight-based FU ( Fig. 10 a), NC50:50’s impacts are lower than those of the alternatives, with TiO2 being slightly less impactful than NC100. From this, we can conclude two interesting facts: the synthesis of NC50:50 is less impactful than that of NC100, and thus, its respective CDs, due to requiring a lower reaction time and incorporating an organic waste source, appear to be more environmentally sustainable than those used for NC100; and that the CDs included in NC50:50 have slightly lower impacts than the bare TiO2, given that substituting 4 wt% TiO2 by those CDs led to a slight decrease of the impact in the three endpoint categories. The smaller impacts associated with NC50:50 are even more pronounced when using a function-based FU (Fig. 10 b). In this case, the FU was calculated by considering the highest rate constant (0.00949 min−1 for NC50:50) and dividing it by the respective rate constants of NC100 (0.00934 min−1) and bare TiO2 (0.0020 min−1). In terms of overall impacts, NC50:50, when compared with bare TiO2 and NC100, presents lower relative environmental impacts due to having the lowest absolute environmental impacts (as observed when using a weight-based FU) while still achieving a considerable increase of the catalytic performance with the addition of the CDs. Thus, NC50:50 is a more environmentally friendlier alternative to bare TiO2 NC100 due to using CDs that require a lower reaction time and are prepared from an organic waste source, resulting in lower the endpoint impacts are diminished to the point that the inclusion of CDs decreases the impacts when compared to bare TiO2. Including corn stover as a carbon precursor for CDs’ fabrication is thus preferable in terms of performance and environmental sustainability.

In this work we have used CDs incorporating organic waste to address the limitations of using TiO2 for the visible-light-driven degradation of MB. To this end, CDs were hydrothermally prepared using corn stover as a co-carbon precursor (with citric acid and the N-dopant ethylenediamine). The CDs were incorporated with TiO2, resulting in a nanocomposite (NC50:50) that presented a better performance under visible-light irradiation coupled to relatively environmental impacts compared to similar composites in the literature.

Regarding catalytic performance, NC50:50 was more efficient than bare TiO2, presenting a % ln (C) of 374 %, achieving a faster and more extensive photodegradation of MB. As for the catalytic mechanism, tests with reactive species scavengers revealed that the superoxide radical, hydroxyl radical, and e-, by that order, are behind the photodegradation mechanism, differing from the results concerning bare TiO2. NC50:50 was also active towards RhB, Orange II, and MO. The optical characterization of NC50:50 revealed that, by adding CDs, we managed to increase the uptake of radiation in the visible range and, at the same time, improve the photonic efficiency, both of which contribute towards the enhanced catalytic performance obtained with NC50:50.

Finally, in a step toward developing a more environmentally sustainable process, we have prepared our CDs in a manner that led to the upcycling of organic waste, in this case by using corn stover, a major agricultural waste, as a carbon precursor. To study whether including these CDs is worth it in terms of environmental impacts, we have compared the environmental impacts associated with producing bare TiO2, NC100, and NC50:50. Both when considering weight-based and function-based FUs, NC50:50 presented the lowest relative environmental impacts, and so, was identified the best option for the photodegradation of MB. This study demonstrates that adding CDs to TiO2 produces nanocomposites with enhanced photocatalytic properties, even when valuing organic waste as a co-carbon precursor, promoting a circular economy. Thus, this work provides a step toward a more environmentally sustainable design of visible-light-driven photocatalysts.
